source.title: A Randomized-Controlled Trial Targeting Cognition in Early Alzheimer's Disease by Improving Sleep with Trazodone (REST).

confidence_rationale: Trial design and rationale paper for the REST randomized controlled trial (NCT05282550, n=100 amnestic MCI, double-blind placebo-controlled crossover) testing trazodone for SWS preservation and cognitive outcome in MCI. This is a protocol description, not results. It supports Q-032's pharmacodynamic-biomarker proposal at the institutional level -- i.e. major sleep-AD research groups treat PSG-measured SWS as a legitimate trial endpoint and the field is operationalising drug-class differential SWS preservation as a testable hypothesis. Held to 0.45 because there are no outcome data yet; this is method-establishing, not result-providing.
failure_signatures: No outcome data. The paper describes the trial design and rationale but no results are available.

Single-drug (trazodone) test cannot adjudicate between architecture-specific cognitive benefit and confounding effects (sedation, antidepressant action, etc.) without further drug-class comparators. A positive REST result would still require replication with a second SWS-promoting agent and contrast against a non-SWS-promoting agent.
Crossover design at n=100 will have limited power for distal cognitive endpoints; the primary outcomes are likely to be PSG-measured SWS and short-term cognitive proxies, not incident MCI conversion.

mapping.source_claim_statement: REST is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover trial (NCT05282550) of trazodone in 100 older adults with amnestic MCI and sleep complaints. The rationale: SWS supports glymphatic clearance and memory consolidation; trazodone has shown promise for inducing SWS in older adults; targeting sleep architecture in the prodromal stage of AD (MCI) represents a candidate disease-modifying strategy. The trial measures effects on both sleep architecture (PSG) and cognition.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-032 limb (b) and (c): supports the architecture-aware-trial-design proposal at the field-level rationale. The REST trial operationalises exactly the question Q-032 asks: does pharmacological preservation of SWS produce differential cognitive outcomes that map onto architecture-specific PSG metrics? Trazodone is the canonical test drug because it is one of the few sleep-promoting agents that preserves rather than suppresses SWS. A positive REST result -- trazodone improves SWS AND improves cognition -- would directly validate PSG SWS as a PD biomarker for trial design. The protocol-level acceptance of this as a fundable, IRB-approvable trial design is itself evidence that the field treats Q-032's premise as live.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This is a protocol description, not results. The REE evidence record cannot count REST as supportive evidence for the PD-biomarker proposal until outcomes are available. What it does support is the secondary claim that the field treats PSG SWS as a legitimate biomarker target -- i.e. the methodological rationale Q-032 needs is broadly accepted in the AD trial community.
mapping.source_context: Trial protocol paper, J Alzheimers Dis 2024, ClinicalTrials.gov NCT05282550, Johns Hopkins / multi-site, double-blind placebo-controlled crossover, n=100 amnestic MCI with sleep complaints, trazodone vs placebo, primary outcomes: PSG-measured SWS and cognitive function.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality moderate because protocol papers are descriptive rather than evidentiary -- the design's IRB approval and funding is the only evidence of field acceptance, no outcomes yet. Mapping fidelity good because the trial directly operationalises Q-032 limb (b)/(c). Transfer risk moderate because trazodone alone cannot adjudicate architecture-specificity (would need contrast against a non-SWS-promoting active comparator) and the crossover design at n=100 has limited power for distal cognitive endpoints.
